title: Egle: The Queen of Serpents
summary: A young woman marries a serpent king and must choose between her husband and her family in Lithuania's greatest folk tale.
full_text:
Egle the Queen of Serpents is the most famous folktale in Lithuania, a story so beloved that its heroine's name is given to girls across the country. It is a story of love, betrayal, and transformation — a story that explains why every tree in the Lithuanian forest has its own character.

Egle was the daughter of a farmer, beautiful and kind. One day, while bathing in a lake, she found a serpent in her clothes. When she tried to remove it, the serpent spoke: "Marry me, Egle, and you will never be sorry." Egle was terrified, but her father insisted she keep her promise to the serpent.

The serpent took Egle to an underwater kingdom beneath the lake. There, he transformed into a handsome young prince named Žilvinas. They married and lived in a golden palace surrounded by gardens of coral. They had three sons and a daughter. Egle was happy.

Years later, Egle's family begged her to visit. Žilvinas agreed but warned: "Do not let them hurt me." Egle returned to her village with her children. Her family was overjoyed, but they also plotted revenge. They did not believe the serpent was really a prince.

Egle's brothers tortured her children until one revealed how to summon Žilvinas. The brothers rowed to the lake and called out: "Žilvinas, come! A deer has stolen your children's clothes!" Žilvinas appeared, and the brothers killed him with scythes.

When Egle learned what had happened, her grief was bottomless. She ran to the lake and called her husband's name. No one answered. The water was red with his blood. Egle cursed her brothers and her family.

The gods took pity on Egle's suffering. They transformed her into a spruce tree, tall and slender, always reaching toward the sky. Her sons became an oak, an ash, and a birch — strong and enduring trees. Her daughter, who had betrayed her father, became a weeping aspen, forever trembling with guilt.

This is why the spruce stands straight and proud, the oak is mighty, the ash is noble, and the aspen trembles at the slightest breeze. When Lithuania was a pagan nation, these trees were sacred. Even today, Lithuanians plant spruce trees in their yards to honor Egle. The story of Egle the Queen of Serpents is the story of every mother, every wife, and every woman who has loved and lost.
moral: Love that crosses boundaries is always at risk; those who cannot accept difference will destroy it.

title: Jurate and Kastytis: The Amber Legend of the Baltic Sea
summary: A sea goddess falls in love with a mortal fisherman, and her shattered amber palace becomes the treasure of the Baltic shore.
full_text:
On the coast of Lithuania, where the Baltic Sea meets the white sand dunes of the Curonian Spit, the waves wash up treasures that have traveled from the depths of time. Amber — the fossilized resin of ancient forests — has been prized by Baltic peoples for thousands of years. And the most beautiful explanation for its origin is the legend of Jūratė and Kastytis.

Jūratė was the goddess of the sea, the most beautiful of all the Baltic deities. She lived in a palace beneath the Baltic Sea, a castle made entirely of amber. Its walls glowed with golden light, its floors were paved with amber tiles, and its domes were studded with amber gems that lit the underwater darkness like captured sunlight.

Kastytis was a mortal fisherman from the village of Šventoji. He was young, handsome, and brave. Every day, he sailed his boat into the Baltic and cast his nets, catching fish that sustained his village. One day, he cast his nets too close to Jūratė's palace.

Jūratė rose from the water, furious. "Mortal," she said, "you have trespassed on my kingdom. You have taken fish that belong to me. You must be punished."

But when she saw Kastytis, her anger melted. He was the most beautiful man she had ever seen. Instead of punishing him, she fell in love.

Jūratė brought Kastytis to her amber palace. They lived together in happiness, the goddess and the fisherman, their love bridging the divide between the divine and the human. Jūratė thought the gods would not notice.

But the thunder god Perkūnas noticed. He was the ruler of the sky, the punisher of those who broke the cosmic laws. A goddess could not love a mortal — it was the most ancient of taboos. Perkūnas hurled a lightning bolt from the sky. It struck the amber palace, shattering it into a million pieces. The walls crumbled, the domes collapsed, and Jūratė's beautiful home was destroyed.

Jūratė was chained to the ruins of her palace at the bottom of the sea, forever mourning her lost love. Kastytis was drowned. But the pieces of amber from Jūratė's palace continue to wash ashore on the beaches of Lithuania and Latvia, carried by the waves, scattered by the storms.

Every piece of amber found on the Baltic coast is a fragment of Jūratė's shattered palace. The small insects and leaves trapped inside the amber are the decorations of her home. The roar of the storm at sea is Jūratė's grief, still crying for Kastytis after thousands of years. When you hold a piece of Baltic amber in your hand, you are touching the ruins of a love story that has never ended.
moral: Some loves are too powerful for this world; they shatter everything around them.
